column2: Four switches that can apply the supply across a load in either polarity — letting you drive a DC motor both ways or generate an AC waveform. Used in motor drivers, inverters, and class-D audio.
Four switches form two legs (half-bridges) between V+ and ground, with the load bridging the two leg midpoints — the shape gives the H.

column1: How do you get forward and reverse in an H-bridge?
column2: Forward: turn on the diagonal Q1 (top-left) and Q4 (bottom-right) so current flows left-to-right through the load. Reverse: turn on the other diagonal Q2 and Q3 so current flows the other way.

column1: How do you set speed and brake an H-bridge?
column2: Speed: PWM the active switches — the duty cycle sets the average voltage hence speed. Brake: turn on both low switches (or both high) to short the motor terminals, dissipating its kinetic energy.

In one leg, the top and bottom switches conduct at the same time, connecting V+ straight to ground through only their tiny on-resistance — an enormous current spike (cross-conduction) that destroys the transistors in microseconds.

column1: Why does shoot-through happen even with correct logic?
column2: Turning a switch off is not instantaneous (gate charge must be removed, BJTs have storage time, gate-driver propagation adds delay). Commanding Q1 off and Q2 on at the same instant leaves Q1 still partly conducting when Q2 turns on — a brief overlap that shorts the rail. The faster you switch, the more it matters.

A deliberate interval inserted between turning one switch in a leg off and the complementary switch on, during which both are off. Make it longer than the worst-case turn-off delay and the outgoing switch is fully off before the incoming one conducts.

Timer-based: STM32 advanced-control timers (TIM1, TIM8) generate complementary PWM with a built-in dead-time generator — the usual route for motor control. Driver-based: many gate-driver/bridge ICs enforce a minimum dead-time (cross-conduction prevention) internally.

column1: How do you size the dead-time?
column2: Just longer than the worst-case turn-off delay (including gate-driver propagation), with margin — e.g. 300 ns for a 200 ns turn-off. Too long wastes part of the period and distorts the output (and loses efficiency), while too short risks shoot-through. Fast, well-characterised switches let you use less.

column1: Why can't software reliably prevent shoot-through?
column2: Even if the commanded states are never both high, the finite turn-off delay causes a brief overlap on every commutation, and software cannot reliably insert sub-microsecond jitter-free gaps. Hardware (an advanced-timer dead-time generator or a protective gate driver) guarantees a both-off guard on every edge, deterministically.
